verdict_reasoning: HSPD1/HSP60 is canonically a mitochondrial matrix chaperonin. However, multiple independent studies document its cell-surface presence: (1) it is detected on the plasma membrane of both normal and malignant cells via flow cytometry and surface-proteomics approaches; (2) tumor cells actively secrete it via exosomes and lipid-raft-dependent pathways, and cancer cells in particular display elevated surface HSP60 acting as a danger signal that stimulates dendritic cells and T-cell anti-tumor responses; (3) surface HSP60 is not simply a passive passenger but engages TLR-2, TLR-4, CD14, and CD40 on immune cells. The surface pool is real but conditional—it is most prominent in oncogenic/stress states—making this a cell_state_induced contextual call rather than a stable baseline surface protein.
